item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oddly enough, the jewel flowers that vanish when touched didn't break my suspension of disbelief. Instead, this did:

Can butterflies do that?
…
The concept was nice but I didn't get it until I read the comments.
No vote for now.

So now I suffer through the summers,
Smile at the swineherd who tells me
Which shoat is off its feed.
Smile at my mother and walk behind her.
My powers have come to me now, and in her candy-colored wake I scatter
Sundew and flytrap, nettles and belladonna.

I smile and wait for November,
For when I come back to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! :3c

Ƹ̵̡Ӝ̵̨̄Ʒ

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been a huge Greek mythology nut since grade school, and the story of Persephone was always one of my favorites. I absolutely love the deft and subtle touch you've used to capture it here. This is smart, and it's got a poetic feel to it despite never breaking SCP format tone.

Possibly my favorite "understated" entry on the site. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too much pure-scary evil-hate-nasty violence-kill-death-maim on the site. The Foundation may have started in creepypasta, but it's branched off into its own mythos, and the quiet anomalies are what I tend to imagine the Foundation encounters the most.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, would it be OK if I tried to re-translate the poem into Greek and see if it works in any ancient Greek poetic meters?

Wow! If you'd like to, then certainly!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You, writer of SCP-2023, just earned all of my Classics-major love. +1

Also, would it be OK if I tried to re-translate the poem into Greek and see if it works in any ancient Greek poetic meters?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
or he would have just followed his brothers example and just left her on a hillside somewhere

…or locked in a tower to be thrown into the sea in a locked chest, or turned into a cow and left in the care of a giant with a hundred eyes by a jealous wife, or turned into a monster with snakes for hair, or turned into a fly and swallowed by her lover, or burnt to a crisp by lightning aura, or…

Click if you want to know names
here there be names

Danaë, Io, Medusa, Metis, Semele. Couples in mythology had it rough, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, how many couples stay exactly the same over twenty years? Two hundred years? how about a couple of millennia? Hades desired her for more than just the initial rape/kidnapping, or he would have just followed his brothers example and just left her on a hillside somewhere knocked up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice work making me think "oh, pretty!" while still having that edge of the eldritch.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate your reading the extra background, then. :)

I admit this did feel on the short side, but I wasn't sure how much more I could add to it without unnecessarily bloating the article. I wanted something like a lighter fare for site-goers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first when I read this, I never really hit a spot that made me look up and hit the plus button. But after I've seen how much work and how much smarts that went into this, THAT is what gave you the vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty much a 180 from how I remember this particular myth going down. I seem to recall a tad more kidnapping and treachery being involved instead of weirdly out of place butterflies and love poetry. Nothing wrong with that of course, but it just didn't resonate with me in any meaningful way. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay! :D I try to write my articles so that there's a sort of "whoa" moment at some point. I'm glad it worked for you!

And yeah, Greek Mythology ftw. I love reading different versions of the classical myths, and there's always so much about the world to think about in the context of the stories (especially given that English has Latin and Greek roots all over the place).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this. I got the ancient Greek vibe from the start. I'm a nut for Greek mythology:)
I didn't get it was Hades and Persephone until the addendum, (that's just me, not the article) and I was like "Oh my Gods! That's amazing! Then the other hints feel into place. And, I'm not sure if others realize this, but Narcissis was a character from mythology, turned into a flower. Scientific humor!!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The inclusion of asphodel especially made me grin.

I recall reading a sort of "modernized, revamped" version of the Greek myths narrated by Hades, when I was in elementary school. The Myth-o-Mania series mentioned asphodel being used for different foods eaten in the Underworld, like asphodel strudel and asphodel and sausage pizza.

I remember really wanting to try asphodel chips with pomegranate dip. I need to find those books again…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
/fistbump

Thanks. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always been a big fan of Greek mythology and very staunchly in the "Hades gets a bad rap" crowd. So have an emphatic +1 from me. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you liked the piece! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mythology, quiet mystery, flowers, butterflies? Sounds like a Zyn skip! I do like your style. Calming and relaxing after hordes of things which could destroy the planet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You like butterflies? That's OK. I just feel like your use of butterfly imagery and (maybe?) symbolism feels too close to your general style. Sure, that's why it's called a style, but I'd love to see you shake it up a bit. Use the other hallmarks of your style: elegant and poetic prose.

Even if you hadn't done butterflies, though, I wouldn't have voted. We got one of these love nature preserve-style SCPs just a few weeks ago, and honestly I'm not in the mood for love. But your writing is so nice it would've been a disservice to down vote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
<3

During the initial draft stage, this was called "Ballad of the First Frost" and I'd written up lyrics to the tune of "Greensleeves". The flowers were going to serenade Persephone when it was time for her to return to the underworld, but I ultimately decided against it because I wanted there to be an aspect of approachability, sort of a "we're seeing the same things they did".

We may never see these gods walk the earth, but we can follow curiously in their unseen footsteps, and examine what they have left behind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, if someone had told me a few months back that the jeweled gardens of Hades and their guardian souls would be a major favorite of mine in this site, I might have laughed, and not in a nice way. I love this greatly, particularly with

At the advent of the first frost, any wind that passes through the area will trigger vibrations in the flowers, resulting in the gems producing melodic tones similar to human vocalizations.

which I find extremely evoking. I like it very much and deserves more than just an upvote. Thanks, Zyn. : )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Butterflies are awesome, and don't let anybody tell you otherwise. You can never have enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
/shrugs

I like butterflies, I like writing things that have butterflies in them, but if the readers feel it's getting too repetitive, I'm not going to blame them.

I do have picture of a shark doodle on a window on a broken door. We'll see where the next article goes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gotcha, thanks.

…I admit I was worried that for a moment you meant the "it was nice, but didn't make me care" aspect made you think I wrote it. >.>;

Looking back, I do need to vary my writing style more. Time to dig out the Sci-Fi DVD collection…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was referring to your addendum where there's a poem carved into a rock explaining what exactly this is in the context of the article, not your statement in the discussion thread.

Oh, I see. Sorry about the misinterpretation on my part, then. Incidentally, the poem was actually two lines shorter when I was having it read over in chat. I added more to it because some readers didn't get the context without the addition. As such, I can't say at that point I would've known to give "a little more credit to the larger audience", since the expansion of the poem seemed to be a useful change. I guess it's a hindsight kind of thing, in this case.

I don't find your concept workable with the classical mythology here, so I'm afraid I don't have much in the way of suggestions.

That's alright. There's not much I can do in this case if the reader happens to prefer the classical myths over the alternate interpretation I have here.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
The mention of Enna and the cave in the first paragraph got me already thinking about the abduction of Persephone myth

Yay! Enna was supposed to be the first hint—although I wasn't sure how much I wanted readers to look it up, given that I'm iffy on the amount of background knowledge that should be required to enjoy reading an SCP. I wanted to have some sort of involvement in the search leading to that one hint, but ultimately I'm unsure about how much work a reader should need to do to have that "whoa" moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I'm… not sure where you're getting this sentiment? Usually my author's notes are there to indicate the little things I've added due to the interesting things I learned during my "research" done while writing the SCP. This isn't necessarily because I'm worried that people would never pick up on them otherwise, but because I wanted to share the details if anyone was interested. I'm going to be frank—I like talking about my writing process, and if not for the discussion page, I'm not sure where else I'd do so. I feel that if someone doesn't care to know more, they don't need to open that collapsible.

I was referring to your addendum where there's a poem carved into a rock explaining what exactly this is in the context of the article, not your statement in the discussion thread.

Given that this aspect seems to vary between views of mythology between readers, I'm disinclined to change the interpretation used here to suit classic mythology, but if you have suggestions as to how to do so, I'd love to hear them.

I don't find your concept workable with the classical mythology here, so I'm afraid I don't have much in the way of suggestions.

Wait, are you referring to in general, or in regards to this article? If it's the latter, could you give some recommendations as to how to present a more genuine concern rather than lust?

I'm referring to Hades in the actual myth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
stupid language of flowers

you're telling me bro
do you know how hard it is to shop for bouquets when you know about the language of flowers
i swear people keep giving me weird looks every time i need to get flowers for orchestra concerts

charming, completely benign sort of skip

This is exactly what I was going for. I still do want to write a piece on the Alcyone myth…

Nicely done.

Thank you. <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm getting the impression that authors live in mortal fear of nobody getting any of their subtle hints about what their article is

Personally, I'm… not sure where you're getting this sentiment? Usually my author's notes are there to indicate the little things I've added due to the interesting things I learned during my "research" done while writing the SCP. This isn't necessarily because I'm worried that people would never pick up on them otherwise, but because I wanted to share the details if anyone was interested. I'm going to be frank—I like talking about my writing process, and if not for the discussion page, I'm not sure where else I'd do so. I feel that if someone doesn't care to know more, they don't need to open that collapsible.

the story has never struck me as particularly romantic or full of feels/ Hades-as-lovelorn-and-concerned-husband just doesn't resonate with me at all

I guess part of it has to do with me being typecast as a "feels author", but naturally, I don't feel that way, hence the existence of this piece. Given that this aspect seems to vary between views of mythology between readers, I'm disinclined to change the interpretation used here to suit classic mythology, but if you have suggestions as to how to do so, I'd love to hear them.

the fact that Hades seems to operate here more on the level of lust and appetite more than concern and tenderness

Wait, are you referring to in general, or in regards to this article? If it's the latter, could you give some recommendations as to how to present a more genuine concern rather than lust?

As a result, what you intended to be the depth of your article serves to detract from it in my reading

I'm sorry it ended up this way for you, then. I'm afraid there's not much I can do about that, since I can't think of a way to have the piece adhere to the classic Rape of Persephone story while preserving the different perspective I'm trying to offer here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: stupid language of flowers I appreciate that this is a very nice, charming, completely benign sort of skip. It tells a nice story, it jibes well with mythology, and while I couldn't quite remember all the particulars of that tale, I still appreciated it while I was reading. Nicely done.

item.title: Re: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded: Personally, I like the way Hades is portrayed in the Dresden Files. That series also says the whole incident with Persephone was really a case of Demeter getting empty nest syndrome, which is funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally, 2023's curse is broken.

The article itself is good. Liked the reference to Greek Mythology. Heartfelt +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Last things first, the ever-present "explanatory note from the skip-maker" really is beginning to detract from my enjoyment of articles. I'm getting the impression that authors live in mortal fear of nobody getting any of their subtle hints about what their article is, and so this has become a recurring feature. Its disguise as a poem isn't really making me forget its function in this case. I'd like to see us as a community start giving a little more credit to the larger audience.

As to the main body of the article, I do appreciate the recognition that pretty crystalline flowers and non-native butterflies is not enough on its own to carry the piece, hence the use of Greek myth as a backdrop.

Unfortunately, I don't really find myself moved by the accompanying sentiment or the interpretation of the Persephone legend here. While there are of course differences in the telling over the centuries, the story has never struck me as particularly romantic or full of feels. Hades finds Persephone, rapes her, and then kidnaps her, causing all sorts of problems in the surface world, to the point where the gods force him to give her back. Of course, he tricks her with the whole pomegranate thing, and as a result she has to spend half the year with Hades still, regardless of her feelings on the subject.

Now, there's nothing stopping you at all from recasting this legend for your own purposes, but at the same time, Hades-as-lovelorn-and-concerned-husband just doesn't resonate with me at all, given the sorrow and death that are traditionally associated with the absence of Persephone and the fact that Hades seems to operate here more on the level of lust and appetite more than concern and tenderness. As a result, what you intended to be the depth of your article serves to detract from it in my reading, and I'm left in the end with pretty flowers. And that's not quite enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Butterflies, feels, generally going for a mood of quiet beauty. This specific article didn't quite work for me, but your style and hallmarks were immediately recognizable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
butterflies are her thing

Bro, do you even booterflies?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Somehow you provide beautiful visuals in a scientific tone. This is really great.

+1

I loved this. The mention of Enna and the cave in the first paragraph got me already thinking about the abduction of Persephone myth, and I enjoyed trying to figure out what was a reference and the symbolism in the following paragraphs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And you just did butterflies.

If this wasn't the case, would you have had a different perception of this article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was pretty sure it was written by Zyn.

Could you expand on what you meant by this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article was good from the very beginning but when I made it the end and realized it was the flower/spring goddess(persaphonie?) that Hades abducted ,I knew this required a upvote! +1

(This would the best SCP to be stationed with to study; it has crystal flowers, butterflies and no adverse side effects yet discovered)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm with Scantron. I like Greek mythology, and the symbolism is nice and well-written, but this just didn't make me care. I was pretty sure it was written by Zyn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't get most of the mythology before I came to the comments, but the imagery was too beautiful for me not to upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The subtlety is astounding, and the imagery concise. Also, you broke the 2023 curse, so that's good.

However, we just saw a flower-nature-preserve from the gods a little while ago. And you just did butterflies. It feels a bit too similar to your modus operandi.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is well-written in every aspect I care to think of, but it ultimately failed to evoke much of a reaction from me. It's just kind of there, no real reason to care about it. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The curse of 2023 has been lifted! Even so, this article was hypnotizing and I loved it! :D +1

item.title: Re: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded:
I vaguely remember some version of the creation myth stating that the gods all picked what they wanted to rule over, and Hades either lost a game of chance or picked the Underworld himself because it was quiet. Either way, I figure that since he's the older brother and all, he's the type to be "sorry guys, gotta balance the books of the dead" when Zeus and Poseidon are all "HEY BRO LET'S GO PARTAY".

Disney Hades is hilarious with his fire hair and smooth talking, but I didn't really like that he wanted to take over Olympus. If anything, Hades tried to avoid getting involved with the problems all the other deities had/made/ended up in.

item.title: Re: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded:
I like Hades as portrayed in the Disney Hercules as kind of a funny guy, I know if I was a trickster god I would at least try and have some fun with it. Maybe Hades isn't bad and being the devil is just the job he got stuck with. I mean you don't have to adore garbage to be a garbage man, you don't have to love to kill to be a soldier.

Ps. FLOWERS = BOOTERFLIES!!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a fan of mythology, I really enjoyed this. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love me some mythology too. This one is truly beautiful, and seamlessly incorporates so many elements of the Hades/Persephone myth. The inclusion of asphodel especially made me grin. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, Dev. I appreciate your straightforwardness in telling me when it was too vague during the draft stage. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the subtlety, and you are absolutely a master of short prose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article is subtle enough to inspire curiosity, but blunt enough for us to get the story. The giant interwoven web of facts you've placed into this enhance the wonderful story further and create a memorable skip with fantastic imagery. Upvoted enthusiastically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this.

item.title: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded:
Because when your wife’s the goddess of spring and a daughter of a major goddess, you can’t just get her a bouquet and chocolates when you need to say sorry. Also, I love me some Greek mythology. :|

Many thanks to Tuomey, PeppersGhost, Devereaux, thedeadlymoose, ProcyonLotor, Light, Eskobar, DrYucatan, Moohab, Kidd, and Thekillerax for reading and reviewing this for me in chat. As always, I’m sorry if I forgot to mention someone here. Edit: Also DEXANOTE, who originally helped me develop the idea waaaay back in… December 2013, maybe. We were thinking of writing a collab but we never got around to it.

Will you have spoilers?
Yes you shall!

I don’t really like how many aspects of pop culture have routinely typecast Hades as the all-around evil manipulative bad guy. He always struck me as just the uptight, stoic oldest brother, who doesn’t really care about the ridiculous stuff his family gets up to but keeps his head level and concerned with his work.

But then again, for all we know, he spoils Persephone rotten and dotes on her more than her mother does. Gotta hand it to the guy, even though his brothers and nephews are pretty messed up in terms of relationships (look at Zeus, for crying out loud), Hades of the myths stays pretty loyal to Persephone even though he sees her only part of the year. She probably still has some enmity towards him for kidnapping her, though, and he’s obviously still apologizing for it. Even the god of wealth has it tough sometimes.

There’s several Easter eggs scattered around for those who are big on Greek mythology.
- Hades is the god of wealth, so all the precious gems and metals of the earth belong to him. Also he doesn’t really have a green thumb, so he tries his best with the flowers.
- About 8 km from Enna is said to be the place where Persephone was originally kidnapped.
- Another name Hades went by is “Aidoneus”.
- “Butterfly” and “soul” are the same word in Greek.
- Some versions of the myth say that the narcissus was the flower attracted Persephone’s attention before Hades kidnapped her. Narcissus is also said to be sacred to Hades.
- Asphodel is said to be the only thing that grows naturally in the underworld.
- When Orpheus played the lyre for him, Hades was said to have wept tears of iron.
- Also this is kinda a random trivia thing, but in the language of flowers, purple hyacinth means “I’m sorry” or “sorrow”, and thyme means “strength and courage”. Persephone might have taught Hades about that sometime, hmm?

Why does everything disappear all the time? Maybe Persephone picks up all the gifts and brings them to show her mother.
